title: Beijing is trying to turn Tokyo toxic in Southeast Asia
gist: Susannah Patton and Euan Graham argue that Beijing's post-November-2025 diplomatic offensive to isolate and stigmatise Japan as a 'neo-militarist' threat will fail to turn Southeast Asian opinion against Tokyo, given Japan's consistently high trust ratings in the region, but will still impose a quiet chilling cost on countries like Vietnam that are more cautious about deepening security ties with Japan.

themes.theme: The rupture and Beijing's pressure campaign

insights.claim: Since November 2025, Japan-China relations have ruptured after China over-reacted to Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's parliamentary statement that a Chinese attack on Taiwan would constitute a 'life-threatening situation' for Japan, with Beijing restricting economic and cultural ties in a wide-ranging pressure campaign to isolate and stigmatise Japan.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Marks the trigger event and scale of the ongoing China-Japan economic/cultural rupture that investors with Japan or China exposure should track for further escalation.
insights.locator: Since November 2025, relations between Japan and China

insights.claim: China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson said in May 2026 that 'all Asia-Pacific countries need to stay clear-eyed and jointly reject Japanese neo-militarism to safeguard the order of peace on which all countries rely to live and thrive,' signalling Beijing's campaign targets regional opinion, not just Japan.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Confirms the campaign's real audience is third countries across the Asia-Pacific, not Tokyo itself — the metric to watch is regional opinion, not bilateral rhetoric.
insights.quote: all Asia-Pacific countries need to stay clear-eyed and jointly reject Japanese neo-militarism to safeguard the order of peace on which all countries rely to live and thrive
insights.locator: As China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson put it in May

insights.claim: China's pressure tactics against Takaichi backfired domestically, contributing to her government's resounding election victory in February 2026 rather than deterring her.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Evidence that Beijing's coercive diplomacy can be politically counterproductive for the target leader — a pattern worth pricing into expectations of future Chinese pressure campaigns.
insights.locator: Takaichi has been undeterred by China's pressure tactics

insights.claim: The authors draw a direct parallel to China's 2020-2022 coercion of Australia, which was largely unsuccessful in changing Canberra's behaviour but had a diplomatic chilling effect on third countries, especially in Southeast Asia — with Beijing now shifting from an 'anti-China/US proxy' framing (used against Australia) to a historical/moral delegitimisation framing against Japan.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Gives a template for reading Beijing's coercive playbook: direct behaviour change usually fails, but the third-country chilling effect is the mechanism that actually works — a repeatable pattern across targets.
insights.locator: There may be a parallel here with China's attempts

themes.theme: Japan's regional trust and security footprint

insights.claim: 2024 Pew Research Centre polling found 81% of respondents in Thailand and the Philippines, 72% of Malaysians and 58% of Singaporeans see Japan as contributing positively to peace and stability, and the 2026 ISEAS State of Southeast Asia Survey shows Japan is by far the most trusted external partner to the region's elites.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Quantifies the trust buffer that makes Beijing's neo-militarism narrative unlikely to gain traction — the baseline against which any future shift in regional opinion should be measured.

insights.claim: Japan has stepped up Official Security Assistance (OSA) and relaxed defence-export restrictions to several ASEAN countries under Takaichi's government, and sent Self-Defence Forces combat troops to the Philippines for the first time to join Exercise Balikatan, enabled by a bilateral Reciprocal Access Agreement complemented by an Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: A concrete escalation in Japan's regional defence footprint — relevant for defence-industrial and alliance-structure watchers tracking Indo-Pacific security integration.
insights.locator: While Japan has been a major provider

insights.claim: Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi has shown greater willingness than his predecessors to embrace a hard security role for Japan while avoiding inflammatory rhetoric, providing OSA grants of defence equipment to the Philippines, Malaysia, Thailand and Indonesia, with potential transfers of used naval escort vessels under discussion.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Names the specific official driving Japan's more assertive but calibrated security posture and the countries receiving material transfers — a watchlist for future naval-asset deals.
insights.locator: Her energetic Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi

insights.claim: At the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, Koizumi pointed out that China 'has a huge arsenal of nuclear weapons and strategic bombers, [whereas] Japan has neither,' undercutting Beijing's neo-militarism framing on its own terms.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: A rhetorical counter that regional elites are likely to find persuasive, further insulating Japan's image from Beijing's messaging.
insights.quote: has a huge arsenal of nuclear weapons and strategic bombers, [whereas] Japan has neither
insights.locator: as Koizumi pointed out at the recent Shangri-La Dialogue

insights.claim: For Vietnam, which carefully balances relations with foreign governments, Beijing's attacks on Tokyo could raise the perceived cost of partnering with Japan, making Hanoi less likely to accept Japanese Official Security Assistance, even though the broader narrative fails to convince the region.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific country most exposed to Beijing's chilling effect — a signal to watch for reduced Vietnam-Japan security cooperation despite Japan's overall high regional trust.
insights.locator: For Vietnam, which carefully seeks to balance

insights.claim: Xi Jinping is reported to be personally invested in the anti-Japan campaign, implying there will be an expectation within Beijing that demonstrable costs on Japan are imposed even indirectly, despite Japan since sending a minister to an APEC-related meeting in Shanghai.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: Personal investment by Xi raises the odds the pressure campaign continues or escalates rather than de-escalating quietly, despite limited Southeast Asian traction.
insights.locator: Chairman Xi Jinping is reported to be personally invested

insights.claim: Japan is balancing its more activist security assistance with economic measures such as the US$10 billion POWERR Asia initiative to support regional energy resilience, and remains appealing to most Southeast Asian countries even as Tokyo's fiscal resources are tightly stretched and uncertainty about the US works in Japan's favour diplomatically.
insights.speaker: Susannah Patton, Euan Graham
insights.so_what: A concrete dollar figure and mechanism (POWERR Asia) for how Japan is underwriting its regional soft power amid fiscal constraints — relevant to Asia energy-resilience financing flows.
insights.locator: Japan's adoption of a more activist approach
